text: The 'attention residue' phenomenon, identified by business school professor Sophie Leroy, explains why even completed tasks can impair focus on what follows. When you switch from Task A to Task B, part of your cognitive bandwidth remains allocated to Task A — rehearsing unresolved threads, reviewing what was left undone. Leroy found that people with high attention residue performed significantly worse on demanding cognitive tasks. The implication is counterintuitive: finishing one thing cleanly before starting another is not a nicety of organization but a prerequisite for full cognitive engagement with whatever comes next.
question: What did Leroy find about people with high attention residue when completing demanding cognitive tasks?

options.text: They made more creative connections between unfinished and new tasks
They performed significantly worse than those with low attention residue
They were more likely to abandon new tasks before completion
They took longer to start new tasks but performed equally well once engaged
